How to double your business in 90 days with the perpetual sales funnel. Before we start, I would just like to mention that the sales funnel means different things to different people. To some it means building an automated machine that you just add traffic to. To others, it means building a well thought out path that leads in prospects travel. How many of you have a process that brings in new clients consistently and predictably and can be demonstrated to be cost effective and shows a positive ROI? I believe that the sales process and ultimately the marketing funnel will determine the success and failure of any small business. 75% of marketers in large organization, those with a thousand employees or more, see generating high quality leads as their number one business challenge. But half of all new establishments survive 5 years or more and about one third survive 10 years or more. As one would expect, the probability of survival increases with the firm's age. Survival rates have changed little over time. Key reasons why small businesses fail. Lack of industry experience, lack of adequate cash flow, poor business planning, management incompetence, ignoring competition, diminished customer base, uncontrolled growth and poor system control. The point is, I believe that a correctly designed and executed sales funnel is the number one most important responsibility of the person in charge. Not because revenue cures all problems, but because it forces you to solve all of the most common reasons small business fail. Planning a business is easy. Generating business is hard. I see too many CEOs and small businesses focused on running the business instead of generating business. Sales funnel 101. Traditional sales funnel. Most sales funnels are some variations of this. You get some leads, you show them an opportunity, you give them a proposal and if you're lucky you get on your clients. Almost all Fortune 500 companies have a sales funnel. For example, this is what their sales funnel looks like. Caterpillar, this is what their sales funnel looks like. Dell, this is what their sales funnel looks like. Coca-Cola, this is what their sales funnel looks like. I can't think of any company of any size that did not have a sales process in place. There may be lots of other things that a company needs, but first it needs cash flow and to do that it needs to sell something. To sell a lot of something, even if it's a high ticket item, it needs a process. The single biggest leverage point in business is usually the sales process. Whatever strategy you implement to improve production, use of product, loyalty or customer spend will usually have its starting point at sales. In other words, if you had 30 days to save your company, the first priority might be to go back to the existing customers and sell them something, but eventually you need to make sales. The one thing every company in the world has in common that is always correct on their annual budget is expenses. At some point, every company has to address low or declining sales or go out of business. Many businesses, particularly small businesses, suffer from irregular sales and so have lumpy cash flow. The solution. The solution to lumpy sales, low sales or how to improve sales is the same. If you get your sales funnel correct, it will smooth out those peaks and valleys. You need to build a perpetual sales machine that builds predictable consistent results. This is how I did it and how my new company is working with other companies to do the same. This was my original sales funnel. I generated leads through affiliates and JV partners and bought traffic, so I had leads, pushed them onto an autoresponder and then I put them on a launch sequence. The priority for me last year in 2014 was to make a profitable sales funnel. When I started, this is an example, we would spend $10,000 on traffic. That would get us approximately 1500 opt-ins. The cost per opt-in was $6.67. We would make four sales for $3,996. Or in other words, we would lose 60% of what we invested. So it wasn't a winning combination. We then started experimenting with the funnels and we had what's commonly known as a trip wire, a $7 trip wire that people could buy. So for the same $10,000 spend, because it's easier to get opt-ins for people selling a lower item product, we got 2,500 opt-ins at an average cost of $4. And this is interesting. So initially we made 225 sales at $7. Then the return path is when the people who are on your list who don't buy, you re-present the offer again. And that's what they call the return path that made another 225 sales. Then we had a level up from the $7, the $47. We made about 22.5. We're getting fractions here because of the numbers, but call it 22 sales. And then again, with the return path, the people who didn't buy that level, we re-presented the offer and another 22. The end result was we lost less money. It was an improvement on the original, but it was still a losing proposition. Fast forward and we made some bigger changes. This time we had three levels. So we would sell a product at $7 and then re-present the product. Then at $47 and then at $497. And that actually showed a profit. So it was only a small profit, 24.91% profits, but it was 152.62% improvement in one month. The next version was we started to tweak a few things. We organized a few things. We added different types of tracking so that we could have a more granular view of what happened. And the end result was we got into profit. So we showed 170.34% profit. So for every $10,000 we spent, we got $17,000 plus back. And from our original starting point, that's over 500% improvement, 583.71% improvement. We discovered that the majority of the money is made after the initial sale during the return path. So the old way of doing things was to put people through a funnel and then you would present them with something and they would either take it or they wouldn't take it. That was kind of the end of it. The new way of doing things is the most important part is that group of people who don't buy, which is always larger. And if you can market to those and get a good result, that's where the money is. The return path is critical. To start with, we show them we buy paid traffic. We send them to a squeeze page or lead magnet. That's where we show them that there's a free report or there's something free or there's valuable information on the other side if they give you their email address. Once they give you their email address, you present them with the $7 tripwire. You make them an offer that's a small denomination, normally under $10. It could be $3, $5, $7, $9. $7 is very popular. It works well for us. If they decide to buy, then you move them to a higher priced offer, what they call the core offer. Same thing again, if they buy the core offer, you move them up one level higher. So that one level higher might be a continuity program. It might be something that they pay monthly. For us, it was a $497 item, I think. And at each stage, you will have people fall out. And you see this along the bottom called the return path. That's always going to be a larger section than the top part. If you continue to market to the bottom part and put people and return them to the original offer or other offers, that's where the majority of the money is, probably 80% of the money. If someone gave you $1.25 every time you gave them a dollar, how many times would you do it? Most organizations are focused on trying to optimize the traditional sales funnel. I believe this is wrong. I think focus should be in optimizing the value of the clients, adding value and extracting value from the clients. That's where the real money is. Sales funnels often look like this. Advertising, core product, hopefully a profit. The new funnel concept is your ad, you make a small, irresistible offer, you make a more irresistible offer, and you make the most irresistible offer. The important thing here is to remember that each level should amplify the first. So if you have a $7 product that you're selling, the next level up should be so irresistible, it makes it easy for the person to continue along the process. That way you make much more profit. This is how we did it. If you can't measure it, don't do it. I often say that when you can measure what you're speaking about and express it in numbers, you know something about it. But when you cannot express it in numbers, your knowledge is often of a meager and unsatisfactory kind. It may be the beginning of knowledge, but you have scarcely in your thoughts advanced to the stage of science, whatever the matter may be. Lord Calvin, basically he's saying measure things in numbers. Every time we bought traffic, we tested three different versions of the opt-in offer. So that squeeze page, that lead magnet, we would always have at least three, sometimes as many as six. So when we sent 100 people, we would send 33 to one version, 33 to the other and 34 to the other one. We even tested different products for the $7 products. We would test three different types of products to see what the market wanted the most. We switched all our long form sales copy, and that was up to 60 pages long, to video sales pitches that are typically around 10 minutes long. Now remember I'm talking about inexpensive products here. For a higher end products, you know, $500, $750, $1,000 and so on, you might need long form sales letters. We use technology by measuring engagement with the videos we could see at what point people stop watching the video. When they rewound, when they paused and when they took action. From that, we could rewrite the script at the weak points. We use the company called Wastea. And here we can see how many times a video was loaded, how many plays it had. We can see when people paused. We can see the engagement. So it's kind of like Groundhog Day. If you make a video and it's not very well received, you can re-script it and redo it until it is well received. We watch where people are clicking. We use heat maps and the moment we're using hotjar. So we watch to see where people are stopping or concentrating their focus. We watch where people look. We can see where people scroll. Again, we're using hotjar for this. We can watch exactly what a person did when they were on a webpage by replaying person by person and watching how the mouse moved, where they looked and what they clicked. You can use Clicktail. We're also using hotjar just now. Heatmap tracker or crazy egg. And you can basically watch what a person does on your page. And I highly recommend you do this. It's very revealing what happens when people get to your page. Here's the big takeaways I want you to get. Everything must be measured. Each step of the process must be frictionless. Must be easy for the person. You have to get inside the prospect's mind. What is the conversation going on inside their head? The majority of the money is earned after the initial sale. Right? So that's what I'm talking about, the return path. So even if people don't buy, that return path is important. If they do buy in the initial sale, what happens next is more important than the initial sale. The focus should be on consumer value, not optimization. If you have a choice between adding or extracting value from your client or trying to optimize, extracting value is probably going to make you more money. Thanks for listening.
